---
title: "Theta*"
---

## Overview

Theta* is an any-angle extension of A*.
Instead of forcing the path to follow grid edges exactly, it tries to connect nodes with straight-line segments when there is clear line of sight.

## When to Use It

Use Theta* when:

- you are planning on a grid map,
- you want smoother, less jagged paths than standard A*,
- and straight-line shortcuts are acceptable in free space.

## Core Idea

Standard A* builds the path one grid neighbor at a time.
Theta* adds a line-of-sight check so that a node may connect directly to its grandparent or another earlier ancestor when no obstacle blocks the straight segment.

This often produces paths that look more natural and closer to continuous-space motion.

## Main Steps

1. Start from the same basic structure as A*.
2. Expand the lowest-priority frontier node.
3. For each neighbor, test whether the current node's parent has direct line of sight to that neighbor.
4. If so, connect the neighbor through that parent instead of through the current node.
5. Continue until the goal is reached.

## Why It Matters

On a grid, A* often creates stair-step paths because it respects the graph edges.
Theta* reduces that artifact by allowing valid straight segments through free space.

## Trade-Offs

- Usually produces shorter and smoother paths than standard A* on the same grid.
- Requires extra line-of-sight checks.
- Still depends on the underlying occupancy grid quality.
- It is more geometric than plain A*, so implementation details matter more.

## How It Relates to A*

- A* searches on graph edges only.
- Theta* keeps the A* search structure but relaxes how parent connections are formed.
- If line-of-sight shortcuts are never available, Theta* behaves much more like A*.

## Demo {#demo}

The demo notebook compares A* and Theta* on the same occupancy grid so you can see how the path shape changes.

If you want the notebook-style workflow, open:

- [Open In Jupyter Lab](http://localhost:8888/lab/tree/notebooks/path-planning/grid-graph/theta_star_demo.ipynb)

Exact steps:

1. Open a terminal at the repository root.
2. Run:

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
jupyter lab --no-browser --port 8888
```

3. Copy the URL shown by Jupyter (it includes a token) and open it in your browser.
4. In Jupyter Lab, open `notebooks/path-planning/grid-graph/theta_star_demo.ipynb` from the file browser.
5. Run cells with Shift+Enter and edit the map or compare path geometry.

Note: `theta_star_demo.html` is a rendered static page. The editable environment is the `.ipynb` file in Jupyter Lab or VS Code notebook editor.
